page.text:
The proof that the axiom of choice implies Zorn's lemma proceeds by a tower
argument. Fix a choice function $f$ on $\mathcal{P}(X) \setminus \{\emptyset\}$.
For each set $A$ of chains one forms $\hat{A}$, the set of elements whose
adjunction keeps $A$ a chain, and defines $g(A) = A \cup \{f(\hat{A} \setminus A)\}$
when $\hat{A} \setminus A \neq \emptyset$ and $g(A) = A$ otherwise, so that $A$ is
maximal exactly when $g(A) = A$. A collection is called a tower if it contains
$\emptyset$, is closed under $g$, and is closed under unions of chains. The
intersection $\tau_0$ of all towers is itself a tower, and a sequence of lemmas
shows every element of $\tau_0$ is comparable, so $\tau_0$ is a chain. Its union
$A$ then satisfies $g(A) = A$, which forces $\hat{A} \setminus A = \emptyset$ and
produces the required maximal element.

"
The proof that the axiom of choice implies Zorn's lemma proceeds by a tower
argument. Fix a choice function $f$ on $\mathcal{P}(X) \setminus \{\emptyset\}$.
For each set $A$ of chains one forms $\hat{A}$, the set of elements whose
adjunction keeps $A$ a chain, and defines $g(A) = A \cup \{f(\hat{A} \setminus A)\}$
when $\hat{A} \setminus A \neq \emptyset$ and $g(A) = A$ otherwise, so that $A$ is
maximal exactly when $g(A) = A$. A collection is called a tower if it contains
$\emptyset$, is closed under $g$, and is closed under unions of chains. The
intersection $\tau_0$ of all towers is itself a tower, and a sequence of lemmas

$A$ then satisfies $g(A) = A$, which forces $\hat{A} \setminus A = \emptyset$ and

"
